positive.comment: Not a stupid question at all, and you’re totally right: Android has had this kind of functionality for years. The big difference is that until iOS 18.2, iPhones didn’t support real-time Caller ID at all. You couldn’t see who was calling while the phone was ringing unless they were in your contacts
positive.parent: Sorry if this is a stupid question, but how is that different from what your phone already does? I'm on Android, so maybe it's different - it warns me of spam callers before I answer. After taking calls I can report them as spam, block numbers, etc all from the default phone app.

positive.comment: The big difference is that Livecaller is completely free, while Truecaller requires a paid subscription for real-time Caller ID on iPhone. We also built it to feel as native as possible on iPhone - setup takes about 30 seconds, no account needed, and we don’t ask for your contacts.
positive.parent: how is it better than truecaller?

positive.comment: Not on iOS. Real-time caller ID on iPhone only became possible with iOS 18.2. Before that, all apps used workarounds. Now they support it too, but only with a paid subscription. Livecaller does it for free, with no account, and is fully privacy-first.
positive.parent: Hasn't TrueCaller been doing this for years?

positive.comment: Not for free on iOS - real-time Caller ID only became possible with iOS 18.2. They support it now too, but with a paid plan. Livecaller does it for free, no account, and privacy-first.
positive.parent: Doesnt truecaller ID already do this?

positive.comment: Truecaller’s been around, but on iPhone it used workarounds like static lists of popular numbers. Real-time Caller ID only became possible with iOS 18.2. Truecaller now supports it too - but only with a paid plan. Livecaller does it for free, with no account and full privacy.
positive.parent: I’m confused about what this is? Hasn’t Truecaller been doing this for years already?

positive.comment: Great question - you’re right that iOS had a Call Identification option before, but here’s the key difference: Before iOS 18.2, apps could only use static, preloaded lists of known popular numbers (especially businesses and spam) - no real-time lookups. What changed in iOS 18.2 is that Apple finally introduced a new Live Caller ID API, which allows apps like Livecaller to identify any number in real time as the phone rings, without needing to preload massive databases.

positive.comment: But where does the server receiving the request get "asdasfG1423SDF" from in the first place to be able to return the "verified number"?
positive.parent: let's say the number that called you is 123456 Iphone will encrypt that to let's say "asdasfG1423SDF" The server will recieve "asdasfG1423SDF" The server will try to find if they have something like "asdasfG1423SDF" The server will return the correct data let's say it returns "Verified number" your iphone will recieve "Verified number" and it will display it Now can the server decrypt "asdasfG1423SDF" to 123456 Short answer: no, because he needs a secret key that he doesn't know This is a simplification but i want to tell you that it is safe and the server cannot know who you are calling, also, i recommend you learn more about encryption algorithms and api's it will make the image much clearer

positive.parent: let's say the number that called you is 123456 Iphone will encrypt that to let's say "asdasfG1423SDF" The server will recieve "asdasfG1423SDF" The server will try to find if they have something like "asdasfG1423SDF" The server will return the correct data let's say it returns "Verified number" your iphone will recieve "Verified number" and it will display it Now can the server decrypt "asdasfG1423SDF" to 123456 Short answer: no, because he needs a secret key that he doesn't know This is a simplification but i want to tell you that it is safe and the server cannot know who you are calling, also, i recommend you learn more about encryption algorithms and api's it will make the image much clearer

positive.comment: I think what you are saying makes sense. It sounds like moreso what is happening is the server does not know who sent the request, not that it will never know what the number is. > The first relay removes the client’s IP address from the incoming request and can’t see the destination address. A third-party provider hosts the second relay. This removes the IP address before it reaches the server. Then, the Apple 3rd party services queries the phone number. Seems like the app creator just hosts a service that answers requests about a number, and does not know much of anything else
positive.parent: > The server will return the correct data let's say it returns "Verified number" If server can know which result it returns, then that will mean it can know what number you queried, right? Apple docs mentions magic is behind a special server implementation named KPIR. It says it's a special server implementation that resolves a query without knowing the search keyword and what records it matches. I'm trying to understand how it works.
quotes: Apple’s Oblivious HTTP and Privacy Pass hide the client’s identity from your server. However, they don’t hide the query itself. Live Caller ID Lookup uses KPIR to hide client queries. KPIR is a cryptographic protocol that calls the server to fetch data corresponding to a particular keyword from the hosted database without revealing the keyword to the server

positive.comment: Thank you for this! I heard about HE before, but I never looked into it. Fascinating! Do you have to use a special database for this? Could you share what you are using or what you have tested?
positive.parent: Using something called homomorphic encryption. If you are interested in the technical aspect of it you can read more about it here:

positive.comment: So, why according to the App Store privacy notices do you collect my contact info as well as usage data?
positive.parent: No, we don’t see who’s calling you or when. iOS 18.2 uses a privacy-first system for Live Caller ID. The Phone app makes encrypted requests to our database, and we return encrypted responses. The entire process uses homomorphic encryption, meaning we never see the actual number or call data. Only your iPhone decrypts the result locally, so we never access your contacts, call logs, or caller info...

positive.parent: No, we don’t see who’s calling you or when. iOS 18.2 uses a privacy-first system for Live Caller ID. The Phone app makes encrypted requests to our database, and we return encrypted responses. The entire process uses homomorphic encryption, meaning we never see the actual number or call data. Only your iPhone decrypts the result locally, so we never access your contacts, call logs, or caller info...

neutral.parent: Passwords are created by the user and immediately (should be) properly encrypted. Server side doesn’t know what the password is. If a company took your password and stored it separately in plaintext and also stored it encrypted - and the encrypted one is what they used for authentication - it would be same scenario as here. You think that encrypted data is safe?

neutral.parent: The 4B+ numbers aren’t stored on the device. When a call comes in, iOS sends an encrypted query to our server, we return an encrypted response, and only your phone decrypts it, so it stays private and up to date. The data comes from crowdsourced reports by millions of [ users over the years (it’s community-driven and constantly updated).

positive.comment: You can only match encrypted data to encrypted data, Iphone automatically encrypts the number before it sends it to the server, Then the server tries to match an encrypted number with another encrypted number to get the results needed

let's say the number that called you is 123456 Iphone will encrypt that to let's say "asdasfG1423SDF" The server will recieve "asdasfG1423SDF" The server will try to find if they have something like "asdasfG1423SDF" The server will return the correct data let's say it returns "Verified number" your iphone will recieve "Verified number" and it will display it Now can the server decrypt "asdasfG1423SDF" to 123456 Short answer: no, because he needs a secret key that he doesn't know This is a simplification but i want to tell you that it is safe and the server cannot know who you are calling, also, i recommend you learn more about encryption algorithms and api's it will make the image much clearer

positive.parent: Nope. Livecaller doesn’t run in the background, so there’s zero impact on battery life. It works through a lightweight iOS extension that only activates briefly when a call comes in. Super efficient and fully handled by the system.

positive.comment: Ah that is great and thank you for explaining it, I wasn't aware of the encryption level in place. My concern would have been if you sent a plain text number, while the app is audited you could store it however you like on a server.
positive.parent: Privacy is at the heart of how Livecaller works. We use Apple’s new Live Caller ID API, which is designed with homomorphic encryption, meaning: - Incoming numbers are encrypted before they’re sent - We perform lookups without ever seeing the actual number - The response is also encrypted and only decrypted on your device No call logs and no contact access. Everything happens in real time, and we’re fully audited by Apple as part of the App Store approval process.

neutral.parent: No, we don’t see who’s calling you or when. iOS 18.2 uses a privacy-first system for Live Caller ID. The Phone app makes encrypted requests to our database, and we return encrypted responses. The entire process uses homomorphic encryption, meaning we never see the actual number or call data. Only your iPhone decrypts the result locally, so we never access your contacts, call logs, or caller info...

neutral.comment: It's not much different than how a spam filter works for email. Each number might have a "trustworthiness" score associated with it. New numbers would probably start with a low trust score and would drop if they started getting reports for that number. A number that's been around for a long time with no reports would likely get a higher score, though, if that number has a high call volume that could lower the score as well. This is all speculation, but that's how I'm guessing the system works in a nutshell.

negative.comment: Right, I get that. But the same logic applies regardless of your area code. You'll still get the same volume of spam calls regardless of what area code you use for your phone number. You don't have to change your phone number to avoid answering spam calls. Just don't answer if the area code matches yours but you don't recognize the number. If it's a real person they'll leave a voicemail.
negative.parent: Spam callers use your phone number's area code, presumably because you are more likely to pick up. Ignore numbers from that area code unless they are contacts and you almost never pick up spam calls.

negative.comment: Passwords aren't encrypted. They are hashed , which means there is no key to decrypt it. Passwords are only verified by comparing 2 different hashes. In a sense, there is no way to compare 2 different encrypted data. However, homomorphic encryptions seem to be able to compare 2 encrypted datas. Haven't read about it in detail, but that is what i seemed to get from the article OP posted.
negative.parent: Passwords are created by the user and immediately (should be) properly encrypted. Server side doesn’t know what the password is. In this case though the billions of numbers are from the company that does the encryption… they can easily find out which encrypted data is the phone number since they have the billions of numbers. Seems disingenuous to say “nah we’d never know” as if it’s technically not possible because of the encryption… it’s entirely possible. Unless I’m reading this wrong. If a company took your password and stored it separately in plaintext and also stored it encrypted - and the encrypted one is what they used for authentication - it would be same scenario as here. You think that encrypted data is safe?
